summary.intent: Audit all utility category components after epic #10 to verify correct implementation, spec compliance, and test coverage, while also splitting the bloated per-category test files into per-component submodules.
summary.outcome: Both utility test mega-files were split into directory-backed modules with 28 per-component submodules each; missing spec-conformance coverage was added (35 total tests) and proptest coverage expanded (44 ignored nightly tests); an audit disposition document was added; all 10 xci-fast gates passed before committing.

repo: Utility test coverage was organized as two category-level mega-files rather than per-component modules: `proptest_state_machines/utility.rs` (2001 lines) and `spec_conformance/utility.rs` (258 lines).

ArsProvider is intentionally adapter/core-owned — its implementations live in `ars-core`, `ars-leptos`, and `ars-dioxus`, not in `ars-components::utility`. Do not create an `ars-components::utility::ars_provider` module unless the spec explicitly requires a shared agnostic type.
The `highlight` utility module is feature-gated behind `#[cfg(feature = "i18n")]` and tests must carry that gate locally in the component file.
New test modules use directory-backed `mod.rs` layout to match repo convention, not single flat files.
Proptest invariant tests are marked `#[ignore = "proptest — nightly extended-proptest job"]` and run with `-- --ignored` to separate them from the default test lane.
`cargo xci-fast` is the final local gate and runs 10 sequential steps: fmt, check, clippy, unit, integration, adapter, spec snippets, error variant coverage, mutual exclusion, snapshot count.
`dx fmt -c` is required by the fast fmt gate for Dioxus files and will reformat files outside the immediate change scope — those hunks must be included in the commit.
Shared proptest strategies reused by multiple utility components belong in `proptest_state_machines/utility/common.rs`; strategies used by only one component belong in that component's file.

code.finding: Former 2001-line mega-file mixing proptest helpers and tests for all utility components; now replaced by utility/mod.rs + 28 component subfiles.
Former 258-line mega-file covering only a subset of utility components; now replaced by utility/mod.rs + 28 component subfiles.

`SetPressed(true)` syncs the pressed value without entering the transient interaction-pressed state, so invariant assertions must check `ctx.pressed.get()` independently from `State::Pressed` — the state enum reflects interaction state, not the logical pressed value.

Python-based file splitting left an extra closing brace at the end of `error_boundary.rs` and `highlight.rs` because the original proptest blocks were moved whole; the parent mod retained delimiter remnants.
Audit disposition document was added here to record an explicit per-entry outcome for all 28 utility spec entries, including ArsProvider's adapter/core-only status.

workflow: When splitting large test files mechanically, use Python scripts that extract sections by exact 1-based line ranges from the git HEAD version of the original file — patching an already-broken intermediate state causes compounding errors.

When submodule names match imported crate module names (e.g., `mod action_group` vs `use ars_components::utility::action_group`), introduce a crate-level alias (`use ars_components::utility as utility_core`) and rewrite all references to the alias before the split compiles.
Sort substitution targets by descending name length when doing token-level `mod::` rewrites to prevent `action_group` being partially rewritten by a shorter `group` rule.
Run baseline tests before any structural change so you have a known-good reference to validate the mechanical split against.

Proptest failures during property test development often indicate incorrect test assumptions rather than production bugs — tighten invariant assertions to match actual machine semantics before concluding there is a bug.

friction: First mechanical split attempt left `proptest!` block delimiters behind in `mod.rs` and produced a naive `action_group::` → `utility_core::action_group::` rewrite that incorrectly touched unrelated identifiers.
The initial Python script patched the already-broken intermediate file state rather than operating on the clean HEAD version, requiring a full regeneration pass.
`error_boundary.rs` and `highlight.rs` each ended with an extra `}` from the proptest block extraction, causing compile failures that required manual trimming.

The `dx fmt` step during `xci-fast` rewrote `crates/ars-dioxus/tests/attr_passthrough.rs` (unrelated to the utility audit); reverting it caused the fmt gate to fail on re-run, so it had to be re-included as incidental formatter churn.
Three separate iteration rounds were needed before the split compiled cleanly, consuming significant wall time on repeated `cargo test` compile cycles.
The ToggleButton proptest surfaced a wrong test assumption about `State::Pressed` vs logical pressed value that required an invariant rewrite.
open_items: Mutation testing against touched state-machine source files was skipped because no agnostic component source was modified in this diff; it should run if future work changes `action_group`, `button`, `drop_zone`, `focus_scope`, `live_region`, `toggle_group`, or `toggle_button` source.

Several per-component proptest files contain only placeholder comments (`ars_provider`, `group`, `heading`, `keyboard`, `landmark`, etc.) where the audit did not identify clear invariants — these should be revisited as those components evolve.

ArsProvider spec-conformance test in `spec_conformance/utility/ars_provider.rs` tests `ArsContext` defaults but does not cover the full provider contract (portal container, root node, disabled/read-only propagation to children) — those are deferred pending adapter-level test infrastructure.

The `spec_conformance/utility/common.rs` helper file was listed as optional in the plan and was not created; if anatomy test helpers are needed by more than two components, it should be introduced then.

No follow-up issue was filed for utility audit findings because all actionable items were addressed in the same PR — if spec drift is found later during adapter work, it should be tracked against the utility category, not reopened as a new epic.
